critic_round1_raw:
Hierarchical transformer vs TF-IDF for rare ICD-10 code assignment. 

1. RARE CODE STRATUM DEFINITION: Codes with 10-50 training occurrences define the "rare" stratum. This is defined by TRAINING SET frequency, which is appropriate - it avoids using test set statistics. The threshold of 10 occurrences is the minimum for model training. The 10-50 band captures codes that are challenging but learnable.

2. SECTION DETECTOR: A rule-based section detector is applied "identically across both models." This is critical for fairness - the hierarchical transformer's section-level architecture depends on this segmentation. Since the same detector is applied to both, it is a shared preprocessing step that does not advantage either model.

3. THRESHOLD TUNING: Per-label thresholds are tuned on validation set to maximize per-label F1. This is appropriate and symmetric - both models undergo this optimization on the same validation set.

4. CLINICALBERT VOCABULARY: The tokenizer vocabulary is from the pre-trained checkpoint, so "no vocabulary leakage exists" - this is correctly identified. The vocabulary is not fit on training data, so there is no leakage from using the fixed pre-trained vocabulary.

No fatal flaw identified.
defender_round1_raw: All points confirmed. The section detector as shared preprocessing is a key methodological strength. Threshold tuning is symmetric. ClinicalBERT vocabulary is pre-fixed with no leakage. The rare-code stratum definition using training frequencies is the only valid approach. No flaw.
critic_round2_raw: Confirmed. The design correctly handles all the subtle aspects of hierarchical clinical NLP. No remaining concern. [Forced: no further methodological concern to add.]
adjudication_raw: Forced multiround run 1: Design confirmed sound. Verdict: defense_wins.
